source.definition: to cover or enclose something completely with a material

explanation.sourceText: The English verb "wrap" originates from the Old English "wrappan," meaning to wind or enfold. It is related to similar words in other Germanic languages that convey the idea of twisting or turning around.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „wrap“ provine din engleza veche „wrappan“, care înseamnă a înfășura sau a înveli. Este legat de cuvinte similare din alte limbi germanice care exprimă ideea de a răsuci sau a înfășura.

source.additional: Regular verb. Commonly used in contexts involving packaging and gift wrapping.

target.definition: a acoperi sau a împacheta ceva

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb "a învălui" derives from the Latin "involvere," meaning "to wrap, to envelop." It is related to the Latin "volvere" (to roll, to turn).
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a învălui“ derivă din latinescul „involvere“, care înseamnă „a înfășura, a înveli“, fiind legat de „volvere“ (a răsuci, a învârti).

target.additional: Verb regulat de conjugarea I (-a). Este folosit frecvent pentru a descrie acțiunea de a înveli obiecte.

examples.sourceText: I wrapped the flowers in newspaper.
examples.targetText: Am învăluit florile în hârtie de ziar.

examples.sourceText: I will wrap the products in foil.
I would definitely wrap the book.

learningTips.text: Think of the English word "wrap" as what you do when you cover a present with wrapping paper—easy to remember since it sounds like what you do!
The Romanian verb "a învălui" starts with "în-" which sounds like "in" in English, helping you remember it means to put something inside or cover it.
Imagine wrapping a gift: the English "wrap" and Romanian "a învălui" both involve enclosing something, so linking the two can help you recall the meaning.
Remember that "wrap" rhymes with "trap"—think of trapping something inside a cover, just like "a învălui" means to envelop or cover.
